title: Why can amphibians regrow limbs when we cannot?
html_body:
Today in Science and science: Penguins monitor PFAS, cleaner ants appear to help other ant species, and more
Brought to you by Danaher Corporation
View this email in your browser
14 April 2026
Today’s Deep Dive delves into some surprisingly helpful ants. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including how some memories can be forgotten (but not lost) and how researchers are turning to penguins for toxicological monitoring.
Neuroscience  |  Neuron
Forgotten but not gone
Why can’t you remember what it was like to be a baby? Adults typically can’t recall episodic memories from the first few years of life, a process known as infantile amnesia. But these early experiences, despite being forgotten, don’t just disappear. They linger into adulthood, even if we can’t consciously access them.

Now, new experiments in mice suggest that some forgotten memories from infancy can be recovered, helping the rodents learn from similar experiences as adults. For the study, baby mice learned to associate a certain context—in this case a box with striped walls lit with dim red light—with a strong electrical shock delivered to their feet. Later, when the mice were adults, scientists reintroduced them to the same context and gave them a single, low-intensity foot shock. While this shock would be too weak to create a new memory in a mouse with no prior experience, these rodents reacted by freezing in place, showing that their forgotten infant memories had been reactivated. Surprisingly, this weak “reminder” also helped the mice learn to freeze in response to a different, related context: a box with geometric wall patterns illuminated with white light.

This new learning during adulthood, the researchers report, involves re-engaging the same areas of the brain that were initially activated during infant learning. “Infant memories,” the team writes, “store information for a long time as memory schemas that support relearning and the formation of new congruent memories in adulthood.”
Read the paper
Ecotoxicology  |  News from Science
Penguins become marine ‘toxicologists,’ thanks to pollutant-detecting anklets
Researchers deploying ankle bands on penguins.  Ralph Vanstreels/University of California, Davis
Along the coast of Argentine Patagonia, Magellanic penguins spend their days shuffling across pebble beaches, plunging into the Atlantic Ocean for anchovies and sardines, and returning to the noisy colonies they call home. But a few dozen of these seabirds have also become marine detectives, sporting a soft silicone band on their ankle that absorbs traces of “forever chemicals”—toxic industrial compounds that can hang around for decades, harming both humans and wildlife.

Already, these unwitting toxicologists are showing promise in tracking forever chemicals, known as per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS). The compounds—used in products ranging from waterproof clothing to food packaging—are resistant to heat, water, and degradation. They persist in the environment for decades and travel long distances by air and sea, posing risks to Patagonia’s coastal species. In a pilot study published last month, 91% of the Magellanic penguins’ bands detected at least one industrial pollutant where the birds swam and nested.

Penguins are well suited to monitor environmental pollution in Patagonia’s waters because they regularly pass through remote marine environments that are difficult for humans to sample. They’re also particularly vulnerable to PFAS exposure because—as top marine predators—the contaminants tend to build up rapidly in their tissues. “We have no better way of understanding the ocean [these animals] live in than letting them tell us the story themselves,” explained study co-author Marcela Uhart. The penguins, she said, “are now our elite team of marine detectives.
Read the full story
Cell Biology  |  Science
Why can amphibians regrow limbs when we cannot?
If you lose a finger or two in a tragic accident, it’s gone for good. But salamanders can lose whole appendages and still grow them back. In a pair of papers in Science, researchers take a couple steps forward to understanding why that is—and towards the eventual goal of giving us the power of regeneration when needed.

When mammals are wounded, our tissues are usually replaced with scar tissue. In one study, a team carefully examined this process by observing how mice heal after partial and total paw amputation. They discovered that if only the tip of a digit is lost, the animals are actually capable of fully regenerating, while deeper cuts scar over. One big difference stood out: Regenerative areas have a lot more hyaluronic acid in the ‘goo’ that surrounds and supports their cells. When they artificially increased this molecule in parts that normally scar, the animals healed better with less scarring.

Another research team took a different tack to examine this question: They compared regeneration in tadpoles and embryonic mice. When they reduced the oxygen in the environment of the young mice, they regenerated far better , while the tadpoles regenerated regardless of the amount of oxygen available. Analyses revealed that the amphibians have naturally lower levels of a low oxygen–sensing protein called hypoxia-inducible factor 1A; boosting this protein in mice improved their regrowth.

Together, the papers show “how modulating extrinsic factors such as oxygen availability and extracellular matrix composition influences the local wound niche,” explained Julia Paoli and Jessica Whited in a related Perspective. “As a field, the way that we piece all of these puzzle pieces together will eventually lead to human limb regeneration,” Whited told Scientific American.
Read the Science Paper  on Hyaluronic acid and ON OXYGEN SENSING
Read the Perspective
SPONSORED
Danaher combines AI-driven discovery with a proven strategic framework to expand what’s possible for the future of healthcare.
Read Now
Deep Dive
In the deserts of southeastern Arizona, several cleaner ants tend to a harvester ant by licking tiny particles off the larger ant’s body.  © Mark Moffett, Minden Pictures
Could these tiny ants be cleaning another species?
Erik Stokstad, Staff Writer, News from Science
Ants will strike up partnerships with other kinds of insects—guarding aphids in exchange for food, for example—but they are almost always aggressive toward other ants. So, it was a surprise to entomologist Mark Moffett when he observed two species getting along quite nicely with a smaller species apparently cleaning a much larger species. In a brief paper in Ecology and Evolution, Moffett proposes that the unusual behavior is the first example of a cleaning symbiosis, or mutualism, among ants.

“It certainly looks like a mutualism, which is quite unusual between ant species,” Joe Parker, an evolutionary biologist at the California Institute of Technology who was not involved, tells ScienceAdviser. “The payoff must be pretty substantial for this interaction to evolve. And that suggests that ant bodies have a bunch of things on them that are nutritious and also problematic if they accumulate to sufficiently high levels.”

Cleaning behavior is well known in nature. Small fish will swim among larger predators and pick off parasites to eat. Birds do the same with rhinoceroses and other large mammals. Even when the larger animal might be able to kill and consume the smaller one, they get a greater benefit by having their parasites removed. The smaller animal gets an easy meal in the process.

Moffett, who works at the National Museum of Natural History in Washington, D.C., noticed the ant behavior while visiting a research station in the Arizona mountains in 2006. He was watching red harvester ants (Pogonomyrmex barbatus) on the ground outside their nest when a few stationary ants caught his attention. Clambering on top were as many as five smaller ants belonging to the genus Dorymyrmex, just a fraction of the size of the 7-millimeter-long harvester ants. The smaller ants walked on and around the larger ants, typically for a minute or so, and seemed to be licking them.

Over the next 5 days, Moffett saw and photographed multiple instances of this behavior. Sometimes, a harvester ant would walk to the entrance of a Dorymyrmex nest and wait for them to climb onto it. With harvester jaws spread open, the small ants would nibble in between them.

Sometimes ants will try to steal food from another species, but the harvester ants weren’t carrying anything to eat. What were they doing? Moffett thinks the Dorymyrmex ants could be removing tiny parasites; even though he couldn’t see any, they might be hiding in places that the harvester ants can’t reach themselves. Another idea is that the ants might be exchanging microbes that are important for health.

Pogonomyrmex barbatus is a well-studied species of the American Southwest, yet no one had reported this interaction before. “Undiscovered fascinating behaviors are still all around us, waiting for a careful observer to note them,” says evolutionary biologist Neil Tsutsui of University of California, Berkeley. “If you see a creature doing something interesting or weird, take lots of good pictures!”
Read the paper
Et Cetera
They keep trying to make fusion happen
The U.S. Department of Energy’s tech development wing, the Advanced Research Projects Agency–Energy (ARPA–E), will provide $135 million in funding for cutting-edge fusion research over the next 18 months, the agency announced on 8 April. That equals the amount ARPA–E spent on fusion over the past 12 years. Many are excited by the injection of funds, however, one physicist cautioned: “We have to be super careful that it’s not more important to just get the money out the door than it is to fund something that will be feasible.”
Read more at ScienceInsider
They’re gonna need a bigger boat fund
Antarctic researchers are thrilled that Trump has included in his 2027 budget request to Congress the building of a new U.S. icebreaker to serve scientists, part of what he calls “restoring American maritime dominance.” Though, the president only asked for $800 million, a far cry from the $2 billion or more it could cost to construct the vessel. “We’re all super excited,” said one scientist. “Although the amount is not consistent with estimates of what the ship will cost, it’s a great start. We’ve been waiting a long time, and I can wait a bit longer.”
Read more at ScienceInsider
‘Meow!’ (translation: not that again!)
Cat owners know the all-too-frustrating phenomenon: A finicky feline will enjoy a particular food for months or even years, then suddenly decide it’s completely inedible. A new study suggests there may be an easy fix: Make it smell different. “Cats may not be ‘picky’ in the human sense, but instead may lose interest when the smell becomes familiar,” explained one of the scientists. To reignite their appetite, one could try “adding a topper, slightly varying the food or refreshing the feeding environment.”
Physiology & Behavior Paper  |  Read more at New Scientist
The limitation is no longer a lack of scientific tools, technologies, governance, policy frameworks, or societal awareness. It is our failure to use them effectively, mobilizing the political will and assuming the collective responsibility to act at the scale the [biodiversity] crisis demands.
Expert Voices  |  9 April 2026  |  Patricia Miloslavich
Last but not least
Happy World Quantum Day! I am celebrating by re-reading Science Senior Editor Jelena Stajic’s wonderful essay on just how much more we have to learn about the quantum world.
Christie Wilcox, Editor, ScienceAdviser

With contributions from Phie Jacobs and Michael Greshko

items.title: 91% of the Magellanic penguins’ bands detected at least one industrial pollutant where the birds swam and nested
Regenerative areas have a lot more hyaluronic acid in the ‘goo’ that surrounds and supports their cells
the unusual behavior is the first example of a cleaning symbiosis, or mutualism, among ants
wonderful essay on just how much more we have to learn about the quantum world
